text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform lateral pelvic node dissection for rectal cancer. Ports are strategically placed above the line joining the right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus. The surgeon clears the area lateral to the internal iliac vasculature, bounded by the external iliac vein and obturator internus muscle laterally, and the branches of internal iliac vessels medially. Posterior rectal mobilization is completed in the TME plane, defining the pelvic hypogastric plexus. Lateral peritoneal cuts are made along the line of Toll and extended over the external iliac vessels, exposing the lateral pelvic node area.

The robotic surgical system is used to perform a lateral pelvic node dissection for rectal cancer. The surgeon inserts ports along a line parallel to the right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus. The dissection begins lateral to the internal iliac vasculature, bounded by the external iliac vein and obturator internus muscle laterally, and the branches of internal iliac vessels medially. The posterior rectal mobilization is completed in the TME plane, exposing the pelvic hypogastric plexus. Lateral peritoneal cuts are made along the line of Toll, extending over the external iliac vessels to expose the lateral pelvic node area.

The surgeon identifies and reflects the ureter, creating the barovar cycle space to provide adequate working space for lateral pelvic node dissection. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to its origin from the internal iliac artery, aiding in the identification of the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon identifies and reflects the ureter, creating the barovar cycle space to provide adequate working space for lateral pelvic node dissection. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to its origin from the internal iliac artery, facilitating identification of the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon dissects and reflects fibrofatty tissue medially along the external iliac vessels, exposing the obturator internus muscle. Further medial mobilization reveals the obturator nerve. Nodal tissue is separated from the internal iliac artery, obliterated umbilical artery, and perivascular fat, and is finally reflected off the obturator nerve as the dissection progresses towards the common iliac bifurcation.

The surgeon dissects and reflects fibrofatty tissue medially along the external iliac vessels, exposing the obturator internus muscle. Further medial mobilization reveals the obturator nerve. Nodal tissue is separated from the internal iliac artery and obliterated umbilical artery, progressing towards the common iliac bifurcation. The nodal tissue is then separated from perivascular fat and reflected off the obturator nerve.
The obturator vessels are preserved during the dissection, with clear visualization of the boundaries of the lateral pelvic node dissection zone.

The surgeon creates a medial space between the pelvic hypogastric plexus and the branches of the internal iliac artery, clearing the node-bearing fibrofatty tissue in this area. The medial zone is bounded laterally by the branches of the internal iliac artery, medially by the pelvic hypogastric plexus, and posteriorly by the sacrum.

The surgeon creates a medial space between the pelvic hypogastric plexus and the branches of the internal iliac artery. The node-bearing fibrofatty tissue in this area is meticulously cleared, revealing the anatomical boundaries of the medial zone, which include the internal iliac artery branches laterally, the pelvic hypogastric plexus medially, and the sacrum posteriorly.
The surgeon identifies the distal limit of the dissection at the first branch of the inferior vesicular artery, marking the boundary for further surgical maneuvers.
